content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

I checked what five websites say about tracking against what they actually do. The gap was bigger than I expected.

5 min read
Just now

--

I built PolicyFixr because I kept running into the same problem. A privacy policy tells you what a company says it does with your data. It does not tell you what the company actually does. Those are two different documents, and most people only ever read the first one, if they read anything at all.
I wanted to test that gap directly instead of just talking about it. So I picked five websites, three Nigerian, two international, opened each one in an incognito browser tab, captured the full network traffic while loading and browsing the page for about a minute, and compared what actually fired against what each site’s own privacy policy claims.

Method
For each site, I did the following:

• Opened the site in an incognito window, no login, no existing cookies.

• Opened DevTools, went to the Network tab, reloaded the page, and browsed normally for roughly a minute, scrolling an article, adding something to a cart, that kind of thing.

• Exported the full capture as a HAR file.

• Parsed every request in the HAR file and extracted the domain each one went to.

• Separated first-party domains, the site’s own infrastructure, from third-party domains, everyone else.

• Read the site’s own published privacy policy and checked whether it named the third parties I found, described them in vague generic language, or said nothing relevant at all.
Five sites: Jumia, PiggyVest, Punch Newspapers, Amazon, and BBC. Three Nigerian platforms and two international ones, so I could compare disclosure quality across both.

What I found, site by site
Jumia. 125 total requests on a single page load. Around 30 distinct third-party domains fired, spanning a real-time ad auction and identity-matching network. Google’s ad stack, TikTok’s tracking pixel, Meta’s Connect script, Microsoft Clarity, six separate Criteo subdomains, and a long list of ad exchanges including PubMatic, Rubicon, Taboola, Outbrain, and id5-sync.com, a service that exists specifically to synchronize a persistent identifier for you across different companies’ ad systems. Jumia’s privacy policy describes none of this by name. It says they use “third-party service providers” and “analytics and search engine providers.” That is the entire disclosure. I also checked Jumia’s own Play Store listing, and its Data Safety declaration states the app may share Personal info, Financial info, and two other categories with third parties, information that is not reflected anywhere in the web privacy policy I read.
PiggyVest. 62 total requests, only 4 distinct third-party domains, and none of them were trackers. Static asset hosting, web fonts, and Cloudflare’s performance beacon. No Google Analytics request fired, no Facebook Pixel, nothing resembling an ad network. This is notable because PiggyVest’s own privacy policy explicitly names Google Analytics and describes exactly how the tracking cookie works. In this capture, the tracker never fired. I want to be precise about what this does and does not prove. One incognito session on the homepage cannot rule out Google Analytics loading elsewhere, after consent, or for logged-in users. What I can say is that on this specific load, the declared tracker was absent.
Punch Newspapers. This is the extreme case. 479 total requests. Only 19 went to Punch’s own domain. 124 distinct third-party domains fired, accounting for 95.8 percent of all network traffic on the page. The list includes the full Google ad stack, Taboola, a dozen Criteo subdomains, OpenX, PubMatic, Rubicon, Amazon’s ad system, Yahoo Analytics, OneSignal, Adform, Sharethrough, Zeotap, Quantcast, Opera Ads, Appier, Smaato, and Temu, the Chinese e-commerce platform, appearing as an ad partner on a Nigerian news site. Punch’s privacy policy says they work with “analytics and search engine providers.” Nothing about 124 companies.
Amazon. 339 requests, only 7 unique domains, and every one of them was Amazon’s own infrastructure. Their image CDN, their internal analytics system called Unagi, and their own ad system. No external third-party trackers fired at all. This does not mean Amazon collects less data than the other sites. It means Amazon is large enough to run its own advertising and analytics stack in-house instead of outsourcing to third-party vendors the way smaller platforms do. Their privacy notice actually describes this first-party advertising infrastructure in reasonable detail, so this is the one case where the policy and the observed traffic line up.
BBC. 161 requests, 30 distinct third-party domains, 54.7 percent of traffic. This sits in the middle. The notable part is that BBC’s own cookie policy names Permutive as their advertising platform, and api.permutive.com fired 11 times in my capture, a real, verified match. Other third parties present included Google’s ad-serving domains, Comscore’s audience measurement service, Chartbeat, Piano’s subscription infrastructure, Optimizely, and DoubleVerify for ad verification. BBC’s policy also names Google Analytics specifically, and while I saw Google’s ad-serving domains firing, I did not see a direct request to google-analytics.com in this particular capture. Close, not exact.

The pattern
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The two Nigerian platforms with the weakest disclosure, Jumia and Punch, are also the two with the most third-party tracking activity. The one Nigerian platform with strong, specific disclosure, PiggyVest, showed the least tracking of any site in the study. That is not a coincidence I can prove causally from five sites, but it is a pattern worth naming plainly.
This is not a new problem I am claiming to have discovered. The Mozilla Foundation ran a similar study in February 2023 across 40 popular Play Store apps and found that nearly 80 percent had discrepancies between their privacy policy and their own Play Store Data Safety declaration. What I did here is a small, manual version of the same question, applied to the web instead of Android, and applied specifically to platforms Nigerian users interact with daily.

Why this matters to me
PolicyFixr started because I kept finding this gap by hand, one app at a time, and got tired of it being invisible to the people using these platforms. This study is the same question at a slightly larger scale, done with raw traffic capture instead of static policy scanning. What I do not have yet is the instrumentation, the statistical rigour, or the scale to do this properly across hundreds or thousands of platforms, the way real measurement research does it. That gap between what I can do by hand and what a proper research methodology can do is exactly why I want to study this formally.

Limitations
Five sites are a small sample. Each capture was a single incognito session, one page load and a minute of browsing, not a sustained multi-session study. Some trackers only fire after cookie consent is accepted, after login, or on specific pages I did not visit. I did not attempt to defeat consent banners or simulate a logged-in user. This is a snapshot, not a comprehensive audit, and I am not presenting it as one.
